1: “So Israel set out with all that was his, and when he reached Beersheba, he offered sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac.” The covenant family descending from Canaan to Egypt — the beginning of the great exile. Beersheba is the southernmost city of Canaan, the last ground of the promised land before the wilderness and Egypt. Jacob stops to sacrifice — to worship at the threshold of departure. The instinct to consecrate the moment of crisis through worship is the pattern of biblical faith: Abraham at the altar on Moriah (Genesis 22), Moses at the burning bush before the Exodus (Exod 3), Jesus praying in Gethsemane before the cross (Luke 22:39-46). The departure into Egypt is not flight but covenant obedience; the sacrifice makes it liturgy rather than mere migration.
2: “God spoke to Israel in a vision at night.” The last theophanic vision to one of the patriarchs — the nocturnal vision that was given to Abraham (Genesis 15:12-17), Isaac (Genesis 26:24), and Jacob (Genesis 28:12-17; Genesis 31:11-13; Genesis 32:24-32; Genesis 35:9-13) is given once more at the threshold of Egypt. The divine double-call: “Jacob! Jacob!” — the same double-call as to Abraham (“Abraham! Abraham!” Genesis 22:11) and to Moses (“Moses, Moses!” Exodus 3:4) and to Samuel (1 Samuel 3:10). The double-call marks a moment of supreme divine address, always at a hinge point of redemptive history. Jacob’s “Here I am” (hinnēnī) is the covenant response — the posture of total availability. Luke 1:38: “I am the Lord’s servant.”
3: “I am God, the God of your father. Do not be afraid to go down to Egypt.” The divine self-identification and the “fear not.” The “do not be afraid” (ʾal-tîrāʾ) formula marks every theophany in the Bible — the presence of the holy God provokes instinctive fear, which the divine word immediately addresses. Luke 1:30: “Do not be afraid, Mary.” Matthew 28:5: “Do not be afraid.” Revelation 1:17: “Do not be afraid. I am the First and the Last.” The “I am God, the God of your father” is the covenant self-identification — not an abstract deity but the specific God who was faithful to Isaac. The God who addresses fear is always the God who can be trusted because of his past faithfulness.
4: “I will go down to Egypt with you, and I will surely bring you back again. And Joseph’s own hand will close your eyes.” Three promises: divine accompaniment (“I will go down with you” = the Immanuel promise of Matthew 1:23; Hebrews 13:5: “never will I leave you; never will I forsake you”); certain return (“I will surely bring you back” = the pattern of the second Exodus, Isaiah 11:11-12; and of the resurrection, Hebrews 11:22); and the comfort of Joseph at the death (“Joseph’s own hand will close your eyes” = the beloved son present at the end). The divine accompaniment into Egypt is the type of the incarnation — “I will go down with you” — the Word becoming flesh and dwelling among us (John 1:14) in the very place of slavery and death.
5: Jacob left Beersheba, and his sons took their father in the carts that Pharaoh had sent. The covenant family carried in Pharaoh’s carts — an irony of divine providence: the very power that will enslave them (Exod 1) provides the transport for their initial descent. The tools of the world used for the covenant purpose: Cyrus the Persian who funds the return from Babylon (Ezra 1:1-4); the Roman census that brings Joseph and Mary to Bethlehem (Luke 2:1-7). The God who uses the instruments of human power for his own ends is the God of sovereign providence over all of history.
6: They took their livestock and possessions acquired in Canaan — the covenant family descending with all they have. The total commitment of the migration: not a scouting party but the whole community, all their goods, all their animals. The completeness of the covenant community’s movement mirrors the completeness of the church’s missionary movement: not partial engagement but total commitment. Philippians 3:7-8: “whatever were gains to me I now consider loss for the sake of Christ.” The family leaves behind Canaan (the promised land) to go to Egypt (the place of slavery) trusting the divine promise of return (v4). The departure from the promised land is the greatest act of faith in the patriarchal narratives.
7: “He took his sons and grandsons, his daughters and granddaughters — all his descendants.” The three generations of the covenant family going down together — the continuity of the covenant across generations. The covenant God is “the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob” (Exodus 3:6; Matthew 22:32) — the God whose faithfulness spans generations. “All his descendants” — the comprehensive scope of covenant solidarity. Acts 2:39: “the promise is for you and your children and for all who are far off.” The covenant family that descends to Egypt together is the type of the church that together “rejoices with those who rejoice and mourns with those who mourn” (Romans 12:15) — a community of shared destiny, not isolated individuals.
8: “These are the names of the sons of Israel who went to Egypt.” The genealogical record begins — the naming of those who are part of the covenant family. John 10:3: “he calls his own sheep by name.” The God who names is the God who knows personally. Revelation 20:12: “the dead were judged according to what they had written in the books.” Every name in the genealogy is known to the God who “calls his own sheep by name.” The genealogy from Reuben through Benjamin is the NT’s twelve tribes: Revelation 7:4-8 — the 144,000 sealed from all the tribes of Israel. Every name matters; the keeping of the names is the keeping of the covenant.
9: The sons of Reuben: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron and Carmi. Reuben the firstborn — the one who lost the firstborn’s rights (Genesis 49:3-4) through his sin, the one who attempted to save Joseph (Genesis 37:21-22) and offered his own sons as guarantee (Genesis 42:37). The tribe of Reuben in the wilderness will settle east of the Jordan (Numbers 32:1). The firstborn who is displaced — the pattern of the divine inversion of natural priority — points to the ultimate firstborn who is not displaced but constitutes the firstborn of all creation (Colossians 1:15-17) and the firstborn from the dead (Colossians 1:18; Revelation 1:5).
10: The sons of Simeon — including “Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman.” The mixed heritage acknowledged in the genealogical record — the son of a Canaanite woman listed among the sons of Israel. The gospel’s non-ethnic inclusivity already present in the genealogy. Matthew 1:3, 5, 6: the genealogy of Jesus includes Tamar (Canaanite), Rahab (Canaanite), Ruth (Moabite), Bathsheba (wife of the Hittite Uriah). The messianic line is never ethnically pure; the covenant has always included those from outside natural Israel. Ephesians 2:19: “you are no longer foreigners and strangers, but fellow citizens with God’s people.”
11: The sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath and Merari. The three branches of the Levitical priestly family — the sons of Levi who will become the tribe of priests in Israel. From Kohath will come Moses and Aaron (Exodus 6:20); from the Levitical line will come the entire sacrificial system that points to Christ. Hebrews 7:11-12: “if perfection could have been attained through the Levitical priesthood… why was there still need for another priest to come?” The three sons of Levi who go to Egypt are the progenitors of the entire Mosaic priestly system — the system that Christ fulfills and surpasses as high priest after the order of Melchizedek (Hebrews 5:10).
12: The sons of Judah — including Perez and Zerah (Er and Onan having died). Perez again — the messianic ancestor, the breach-maker (Genesis 38:29) who was born ahead of the scarlet thread. Ruth 4:18-22 traces the line: Perez → Hezron → … → David. Matthew 1:3 names Perez explicitly in the genealogy of Christ. The genealogy of those who went to Egypt includes the exact ancestor through whom the Savior will come. The covenant family descending into slavery carries within it the Messianic seed: Judah son of Jacob, Perez son of Judah, … David, … Jesus. Egypt does not terminate the messianic line; it preserves it.
13: The sons of Issachar — four names, unremarkable. The anonymous faithfulness of the covenant family: those whose names appear in a list but not in any narrative of heroism or failure. 1 Corinthians 12:22: “those parts of the body that seem to be weaker are indispensable.” The tribes that never have a major narrative role (Issachar, Zebulun, Gad) are nonetheless counted, named, and present in the covenant community. The gospel’s valuation of the unnamed: Luke 14:21 — the poor, the crippled, the blind, the lame brought into the banquet. Every name in the list of those who went to Egypt is known to God.
14: The sons of Zebulun — three names. Zebulun whose territory will include “Galilee of the Gentiles” (Isaiah 9:1; Matthew 4:15). Matthew 4:12-16: Jesus withdraws to Galilee, to the territory of Zebulun and Naphtali, fulfilling Isaiah’s prophecy. The descendants of Zebulun who go to Egypt will settle in the land that becomes the heartland of Jesus’ Galilean ministry. The geography of the covenant family’s settlement in the promised land is the geography of Jesus’ earthly ministry. The light that rises on Zebulun (Isaiah 9:2) is the light of the world (John 8:12) who ministers in Zebulun’s territory.
15: Thirty-three sons and daughters of Leah — the firstborn wife’s descendants. The numerical summary marks the completeness of the group: 33 from Leah, 14 from Rachel (v22), 16 from Zilpah (v18), 7 from Bilhah (v25) = 70 total (vv26-27). The number 70 is not accidental: Gen 10 lists 70 nations; Luke 10:1-17 records Jesus sending out 70 disciples into “every town and place.” The 70 who go to Egypt mirror the 70 nations of the world; the 70 disciples mirror the 70 who went to Egypt. The covenant family is numbered like the nations — the microcosm that carries the promise of blessing to the macrocosm (Genesis 12:3: “all peoples on earth will be blessed through you”).
16: The sons of Gad — seven sons listed. Gad whose name means “fortune” or “a troop” (Genesis 30:11). The tribe of Gad will settle east of the Jordan (Numbers 32:1) and will cross armed ahead of the rest of Israel into the promised land (Numbers 32:17). The willingness to fight for what the whole community inherits — “I will fight for you” — is the type of Christ who goes before the church into the battle, as the pioneer and perfecter of faith (Hebrews 12:2). Every name in every tribe is known to “him who knows the name of every star” (Psalms 147:4) and calls his own sheep by name (John 10:3).
17: The sons of Asher including “their sister Serah.” Serah is one of the few women named in tribal genealogies — named and counted in the 70, present in the covenant community’s descent into Egypt. Later tradition (Numbers 26:46; 1 Chronicles 7:30) also records Serah as Asher’s daughter. Galatians 3:28: “there is neither Jew nor Gentile, neither slave nor free, nor is there male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus.” The naming of Serah in the genealogy is a small mark of the covenant community’s inclusion of women in its formal registry — the same inclusion that the gospel extends fully. The daughters of God are counted in the family.
18: Sixteen from Zilpah — the children born through the concubine Laban gave to Leah. The covenant community includes those born through the concubines, not only through the primary wives. The inclusion of Zilpah’s children in the covenant number (included in the 70) is the type of the gospel’s inclusion of all categories of persons. Galatians 3:28. The 16 from Zilpah are fully counted, fully named, fully present. The God whose covenant encompasses the marginal — the concubine’s children — is the God who sees Hagar in the wilderness (Genesis 16:13: “You are the God who sees me”) and who receives the Gentiles as “fellow heirs, members of the same body” (Ephesians 3:6).
19: The sons of Jacob’s wife Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin. The two sons of the beloved wife — the two whom Jacob loved most. Joseph and Benjamin are the last named because they are the most beloved. The order of love — Leah first (the less beloved), Rachel last (the beloved) — is the order of divine election reversed from human preference: “the first will be last and the last will be first” (Matthew 20:16). But both are named, both counted, both present. The divine love encompasses both the apparently favored and the apparently disadvantaged — Leah’s tears were seen (Genesis 29:31: “the LORD saw that Leah was not loved”) as clearly as Rachel’s joy.
20: In Egypt, Manasseh and Ephraim were born to Joseph by Asenath. The two sons born in Egypt — the children of the exiled viceroy and his Gentile bride. They were already present (Genesis 41); they are listed here because they are included in the 70. The Gentile bride’s children counted among the covenant family — the inclusion of the ethnically mixed in the official genealogy. This prefigures the Gentile nations being grafted into the covenant: Romans 11:17-18 — the wild olive branches grafted in. Asenath’s sons becoming tribal patriarchs of Israel is the type of the Gentile church becoming co-heirs with Israel (Ephesians 3:6).
21: The sons of Benjamin — ten sons listed. Benjamin the youngest, the most protected, the one Jacob was unwilling to let go (Genesis 42:38). Ten sons from the youngest — the multiplication of the one who was withheld out of fear. The God who preserves and multiplies the beloved: “he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all — how will he not also, along with him, graciously give us all things?” (Romans 8:32). Benjamin’s ten sons in Egypt are the sign that the withheld beloved, when finally surrendered to the journey, becomes multiply fruitful. The surrender of the beloved is always the condition of covenant multiplication.
22: Fourteen from Rachel — the children of the beloved wife, including Joseph’s two sons in Egypt. The beloved wife’s 14 descendants are the core of the covenant’s most beloved line: Joseph the type of Christ, Benjamin the last beloved, and their 12 sons between them. The number 14 appears in the genealogy of Jesus (Matthew 1:17: “fourteen generations from Abraham to David, fourteen from David to the exile, fourteen from the exile to the Christ”). Whether coincidental or intentional, the 14 of Rachel echo the 14 of the Matthean genealogy that culminates in the one Rachel’s children prefigured. The beloved line produces the beloved Son.
23: The son of Dan: Hushim — only one son listed for Dan. The single descendant of the firstborn of the concubines. Dan is one of the twelve tribes but conspicuously absent from the 144,000 sealed in Revelation 7:4-8 (replaced by Manasseh). The tribe of Dan is associated with idolatry in the book of Judges (Judg 18). The single son going to Egypt is not a sign of God’s abandonment but of the mysterious asymmetry of the covenant — some lines are fuller, some thinner, but all are kept. The God who keeps the single son of Dan keeps the single lost sheep (Luke 15:4-6) with the same care as the 99.
24: The sons of Naphtali: four names — Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer and Shillem. Naphtali, whose territory in the north includes “Galilee of the Gentiles” (Isaiah 9:1) alongside Zebulun. Matthew 4:15-16: “land of Zebulun and land of Naphtali… in Galilee of the Gentiles — the people living in darkness have seen a great light.” The four sons of Naphtali who go to Egypt are the progenitors of the tribe that will see the great light. The geography of descent (from Canaan to Egypt) and the geography of ascent (return to Canaan, and later Jesus’ Galilean ministry in the territory of Naphtali) are both within the divine purpose.
25: Seven from Bilhah — the children of Rachel’s concubine. The covenant family’s 70 are constituted of four women’s children: Leah (33), Rachel (14), Zilpah (16), Bilhah (7). The four women whose children make up the covenant community — two wives, two concubines. The covenant encompasses all categories of descent. 2 Samuel 22:7 — “from his temple he heard my voice.” The God who hears from every quarter — the God who “formed my inward parts” of each of the 70 souls in the list (Psalms 139:13) — is the God who will send his Son to be the savior of every soul thus formed.
26: Sixty-six direct descendants of Jacob who went to Egypt — not counting his sons’ wives. The accounting precision of the text — 66 without the wives, 70 with Joseph’s two sons already in Egypt. The God who counts precisely: Luke 12:7 — “even the very hairs of your head are all numbered.” The 66 who cross from Canaan to Egypt are known individually, not as an undifferentiated mass. The precision of the count is the precision of divine care — “not one of them will fall to the ground outside your Father’s care” (Matthew 10:29).
27: “With the two sons who had been born to Joseph in Egypt, the members of Jacob’s family who went to Egypt totaled seventy.” Seventy — the number of the nations (Gen 10) and of Jesus’ disciples (Luke 10:1). The covenant family, now as numerous as the world’s nations, enters the world (Egypt) to grow and to multiply. Acts 1:8: “you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth.” The 70 who go to Egypt are the seed of the 600,000 who will come out (Exodus 12:37); the 70 disciples who go out are the seed of the church that will cover the earth. Seventy is the number of universal scope.
28: “Jacob had sent Judah ahead of him to Joseph.” Judah as the forerunner — sent ahead to prepare the way before the father’s arrival. The pattern of the forerunner: Malachi 3:1 — “I will send my messenger, who will prepare the way before me.” Matthew 11:10 — John the Baptist as the forerunner who prepares the way before Christ. Judah goes before Jacob as John goes before Jesus. The forerunner who prepares the meeting between the father and the beloved son: the function of the covenant’s prophetic tradition is always to prepare the world for the arrival of the one who comes after. The forerunner’s task is to get directions — to make the meeting possible.
29: Joseph had his chariot made ready and went to meet his father in Goshen. “As soon as Joseph appeared before him, he threw his arms around his father and wept for a long time.” The viceroy of Egypt running to embrace his aged father — the complete abandonment of dignity in the name of love. Luke 15:20: “while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and was filled with compassion for him; he ran to his son, threw his arms around him and kissed him.” The roles are reversed here: it is the exalted son who runs to the aged father. But the weeping and the embrace are the same as Luke 15 — the reunion of those who were separated by the apparent death of the beloved. Joseph’s tears are the tears of the homecoming.
30: “Now I am ready to die, since I have seen for myself that you are still alive.” The Nunc Dimittis of the patriarchs: the old man ready to depart in peace having seen the beloved son he thought dead. Luke 2:29-30: “Sovereign Lord, as you have promised, you may now dismiss your servant in peace. For my eyes have seen your salvation.” Simeon’s Nunc Dimittis (having seen the Christ-child) is Jacob’s Nunc Dimittis in miniature (having seen the son he thought dead). Both old men are released from the fear of death by the sight of the beloved. Death has no sting when the loved one is seen alive — which is the gospel’s answer to death: “because I live, you also will live” (John 14:19).
31: Joseph announces he will go to Pharaoh to present his family — “My brothers and my father’s household have come to me.” The viceroy presenting his family to the king — the mediatorial function. Hebrews 2:11: “Jesus is not ashamed to call them brothers.” The one who sits at the right hand of Pharaoh goes to Pharaoh on behalf of his brothers — just as the one at the right hand of the Father intercedes before the Father on behalf of his brothers. The intercession of the viceroy: “I will speak to Pharaoh for you” is the type of “he always lives to intercede for them” (Hebrews 7:25).
32: “The men are shepherds; they tend livestock.” Joseph describing his brothers as shepherds — the occupation that is “detestable to the Egyptians” (v34). The covenant family is identified by an occupation that marks them as alien, marginal, and despised in Egypt. 1 Corinthians 1:27-28: “God chose the foolish things of the world to shame the wise… the lowly things of this world and the despised things.” The Good Shepherd (John 10:11) comes from a family of shepherds who were despised in Egypt. The vocation that the world despises is the vocation through which the world’s salvation comes.
33: “When Pharaoh calls you in and asks, ‘What is your occupation?’” Joseph prepares his brothers for the interview with Pharaoh — the coaching of the unprepared. The patient preparation of the inexperienced: John 16:1-4: “All this I have told you so that you will not fall away.” Christ prepares his disciples for what is coming — the questions they will be asked, the opposition they will face, the answers they should give. Joseph’s preparatory coaching is the type of the Spirit’s preparatory work: “the Advocate, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, will teach you all things and will remind you of everything I have said to you” (John 14:26).
34: “You should answer, ‘Your servants have tended livestock from our boyhood on.’ Then you will be allowed to settle in Goshen, for all shepherds are detestable to the Egyptians.” The strategy of honest self-identification — owning the marginalized occupation in order to receive the best land (Goshen). The paradox: the detestable occupation is precisely what secures the separate settlement. 1 Corinthians 4:10: “we are fools for Christ… we are weak… we are dishonored.” The church’s marginalized identity in the world is precisely what preserves its distinctiveness. The shepherds who are despised by Egypt are given Goshen — the best land, separate from Egypt, where they can multiply and preserve their identity. Separation through marginalization is the pattern of covenant preservation.
